Bounding.label: thm:monotonicity
Bounding.name: Monotonicity of Supremum and Infimum
Bounding.question: State Monotonicity of Supremum and Infimum.
Bounding.answer: If $A\subseteq B$ (nonempty, bounded): $\sup A\le\sup B$ and $\inf B\le\inf A$.
Bounding.hint: Every upper bound of $B$ is also an upper bound of $A$.

Bounding.label: thm:monotonicity
Bounding.name: Monotonicity of Supremum and Infimum
Bounding.question: Give a counterexample or failure case for: Monotonicity of Supremum and Infimum.
Bounding.answer: $A\subseteq B$ does not force strict inequality: equality is possible.

Bounding.label: thm:monotonicity
Bounding.name: Monotonicity of Supremum and Infimum
Bounding.question: What does Monotonicity of Supremum and Infimum immediately give you?
Bounding.answer: Diameter Monotonicity

Bounding.label: thm:monotonicity-functions
Bounding.name: Monotonicity for Functions
Bounding.question: State Monotonicity of Supremum for functions.
Bounding.answer: If $A\subseteq B\subseteq X$: $\sup_{A}f\le\sup_{B}f$ and $\inf_{B}f\le\inf_{A}f$.
Bounding.hint: Restricting the domain can only shrink or preserve extrema.

Bounding.label: cor:diam-monotone
Bounding.name: Diameter Monotonicity
Bounding.question: State Diameter Monotonicity.
Bounding.answer: In metric space $(X,d)$: $A\subseteq B\Rightarrow\operatorname{diam}(A)\le\operatorname{diam}(B)$.
Bounding.hint: Diameter = supremum of distances; monotonicity of sup applies.

Bounding.label: cor:norm-bounds
Bounding.name: Norm Bounds
Bounding.question: State the corollary: Norm Bounds.
Bounding.answer:
Let $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ with $A \subseteq B$. Then
\[
\sup_{x\in A} \|x\|
\le
\sup_{x\in B} \|x\|.
\]

Bounding.label: thm:neg-rule
Bounding.name: Negation Rule
Bounding.question: State the Negation Rule for sup/inf.
Bounding.answer: For nonempty bounded $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$: $\sup(-A)=-\inf A$ and $\inf(-A)=-\sup A$.
Bounding.hint: Negation flips the order; the new least upper bound is the negation of the old greatest lower bound.

Bounding.label: thm:neg-rule
Bounding.name: Negation Rule
Bounding.question: What does Negation Rule immediately give you?
Bounding.answer: Scalar Multiplication ($c < 0$)

Bounding.label: thm:sup-add
Bounding.name: Additivity of Supremum
Bounding.question: State Additivity of Supremum.
Bounding.answer: $\sup(A+B)=\sup A+\sup B$ for nonempty bounded $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$.
Bounding.hint: Show $\sup A+\sup B$ is an upper bound; use $\varepsilon$-characterisation to show tightness.

Bounding.label: thm:inf-add
Bounding.name: Additivity of Infimum
Bounding.question: State Additivity of Infimum.
Bounding.answer: $\inf(A+B)=\inf A+\inf B$ for nonempty bounded $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$.

Bounding.label: thm:scale-pos
Bounding.name: Scalar Multiplication ($c > 0$)
Bounding.question: State scalar multiplication for sup/inf ($c>0$).
Bounding.answer: For $c>0$: $\sup(cA)=c\sup A$ and $\inf(cA)=c\inf A$.
Bounding.hint: Scaling by $c>0$ preserves order; extrema scale by $c$.

Bounding.label: thm:scale-neg
Bounding.name: Scalar Multiplication ($c < 0$)
Bounding.question: State scalar multiplication for sup/inf ($c<0$).
Bounding.answer: For $c<0$: $\sup(cA)=c\inf A$ and $\inf(cA)=c\sup A$.
Bounding.hint: Scaling by $c<0$ reverses order; sup and inf swap roles.

Bounding.label: thm:sup-diff
Bounding.name: Supremum of a Difference
Bounding.question: State the Supremum of a Difference.
Bounding.answer: $\sup(A - B) = \sup A - \inf B$ and $\inf(A-B) = \inf A - \sup B$.
Bounding.hint: $A - B = A + (-B)$; apply additivity then negation rule.

Bounding.label: thm:sup-diff
Bounding.name: Supremum of a Difference
Bounding.question:
$\forall\, A, B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ nonempty and bounded:
$\sup\{a - b : a \in A,\, b \in B\} = \sup A - \inf B$.
Bounding.answer: This is the Theorem: Supremum of a Difference

Bounding.label: thm:sup-diff
Bounding.name: Supremum of a Difference
Bounding.question: Give a counterexample or failure case for: Supremum of a Difference.
Bounding.answer: Common error: $\sup(A-B) = \sup A - \sup B$. To maximise $a - b$, minimise $b$, so $\inf B$, not $\sup B$.

Bounding.label: prop:sup-unique
Bounding.name: Uniqueness of Supremum and Infimum
Bounding.question: State uniqueness of supremum and infimum.
Bounding.answer: If $\sup A$ exists it is unique; if $\inf A$ exists it is unique.
Bounding.hint: If $s,s'$ are both suprema: $s\le s'$ and $s'\le s$, so $s=s'$.

Bounding.label: prop:eps-char
Bounding.name: $\varepsilon$-Characterization of Supremum and Infimum
Bounding.question: State the $\varepsilon$-characterisation of supremum.
Bounding.answer: $s=\sup A$ iff: (i) $s$ is an upper bound of $A$; and (ii) $\forall\varepsilon>0\,\exists a\in A: a>s-\varepsilon$.
Bounding.hint: The $\varepsilon$-test replaces abstract least-ness with a constructive approximation condition.

Bounding.label: prop:eps-char
Bounding.name: $\varepsilon$-Characterization of Supremum and Infimum
Bounding.question: What does $\varepsilon$-Characterization of Supremum and Infimum immediately give you?
Bounding.answer: $\varepsilon$-Approximation

Bounding.label: cor:eps-approx
Bounding.name: $\varepsilon$-Approximation
Bounding.question: State the $\varepsilon$-Approximation Corollary.
Bounding.answer: If $s = \sup A$, then $\forall \varepsilon > 0\; \exists a \in A$: $s - \varepsilon < a \leq s$.
Bounding.hint: Direct restatement of the $\varepsilon$-characterization with the upper bound condition made explicit.

Bounding.label: thm:nested-interval-property
Bounding.name: Nested Interval Property
Bounding.question: State the Nested Interval Property.
Bounding.answer: If $[a_1,b_1]\supseteq[a_2,b_2]\supseteq\cdots$ are nonempty closed intervals, then $\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty[a_n,b_n]\neq\varnothing$.
Bounding.hint: $(a_n)$ is increasing and bounded above; $s=\sup\{a_n\}$ lies in every interval.

Bounding.label: thm:nested-interval-property
Bounding.name: Nested Interval Property
Bounding.question: Give a counterexample or failure case for: Nested Interval Property.
Bounding.answer: Fails for open intervals: $\bigcap_n(0,1/n)=\varnothing$.

Bounding.label: thm:archimedean-property
Bounding.name: Archimedean Property
Bounding.question: State the Archimedean Property.
Bounding.answer: For any $x\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ with $n>x$. Equivalently, $\mathbb{N}$ is unbounded in $\mathbb{R}$.
Bounding.hint: If $\mathbb{N}$ were bounded, $\sup\mathbb{N}$ would exist; but $\sup\mathbb{N}-1$ is not an upper bound, contradiction.

Bounding.label: thm:archimedean-property
Bounding.name: Archimedean Property
Bounding.question: What does Archimedean Property immediately give you?
Bounding.answer: Floor Lemma, Density of $\mathbb{Q}$

Bounding.label: thm:density-of-rationals
Bounding.name: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$
Bounding.question: State density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$.
Bounding.answer: $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$: for any $a<b$ there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $a<q<b$.
Bounding.hint: Use Archimedean to find $n$ with $1/n<b-a$; then $m=\lfloor na\rfloor+1$ gives the numerator $q=m/n$.

Bounding.label: thm:density-of-rationals
Bounding.name: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$
Bounding.question: What does Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ immediately give you?
Bounding.answer: Density of Irrationals

Bounding.label: thm:existence-of-square-roots
Bounding.name: Existence of Square Roots
Bounding.question: State the Existence and Uniqueness of Square Roots.
Bounding.answer: $\forall a \geq 0\; \exists!\, x \geq 0$: $x^2 = a$.
Bounding.hint: Existence: let $S = \{t \geq 0 : t^2 \leq a\}$; show $x = \sup S$ satisfies $x^2 = a$ by ruling out $x^2 < a$ and $x^2 > a$ via $\varepsilon$-characterization.

Bounding.label: thm:existence-of-square-roots
Bounding.name: Existence of Square Roots
Bounding.question: Give a counterexample or failure case for: Existence of Square Roots.
Bounding.answer: $\mathbb{Q}$ fails: $\{r \in \mathbb{Q} : r^2 < 2\}$ is bounded but has no rational supremum.

Bounding.label: lem:floor-lemma
Bounding.name: Floor Lemma
Bounding.question: State the Floor Lemma.
Bounding.answer: For every $x\in\mathbb{R}$ there is a unique $m\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $m\le x<m+1$.
Bounding.hint: Existence from Archimedean; uniqueness since two consecutive integers cannot both satisfy the condition.

Bounding.label: lem:floor-lemma
Bounding.name: Floor Lemma
Bounding.question: What does Floor Lemma immediately give you?
Bounding.answer: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$

Bounding.label: cor:density-of-irrationals
Bounding.name: Density of Irrationals
Bounding.question: State the corollary: Density of Irrationals.
Bounding.answer: The irrationals are dense in $\mathbb{R}$.
